text: ‍“You can’t scale your data without scaling trust.” from “Data Mesh: Delivering Data-Driven Value at Scale” by Zhamak Dehghani  As organizations evolve into federated, domain-oriented structures, the promise of scalable, self-serve data products becomes both powerful and complex. But what happens when those products overlap, contradict, or serve competing interpretations of the same business entity? When data products collide, the core question becomes: Who owns the truth?

doc_name: ingested\Cross-domain delivery.txt
text: When data products collide, the core question becomes: Who owns the truth? This is not a failure, it’s a signal of maturity. It indicates your ecosystem is growing, your teams are engaged, and your domains are striving to deliver value.

doc_name: ingested\Cross-domain delivery.txt

doc_name: ingested\Cross-domain delivery.txt
text: In a cross-domain environment, truth isn’t static. It’s contextual. The focus has moved heavily toward agile delivery and domain-driven design, yet the silent role of shared codes, lookups, and hierarchies in ensuring consistent insight is still underestimated. Without a reliable reference layer, even the most sophisticated data products risk delivering fractured truths.

text: The most advanced platforms and domain data products cannot compensate for a broken semantic foundation, treating reference data as enterprise infrastructure with real ownership, enforced change control, and measurable impact is no longer optional. Reference data is a prerequisite for scaling trusted analytics, responsible AI, and sustainable federated delivery. Without clear enterprise ownership and consequence for semantic breakage, reference governance degrades into documentation instead of control.
